contradiction_text: Headline pass rates are presented as performance outcomes, but GPT-5's low score coincides with extremely high non-finish and early-stop rates, making the result hard to interpret as a pure code-synthesis measure.
related_claim_texts: Reported end-to-end performance remains limited in this setting, with the best overall pass rate at 40.2% for Claude-Sonnet-4.5 using Claude Code, while GPT-5 is reported at 21.7%.

{'The paper’s own results suggest that execution dynamics and task visibility materially affect outcomes': 'GPT-5 and Qwen3-Thinking show high non-finish or early-stop rates, task_tracker usage is strongly correlated with performance, and revealing all test cases substantially improves Claude-Sonnet-4.5 results.'}
disclosure: GPT-5's 21.7% score should not be read as a clean capability ranking because the paper also reports failure-to-finish behavior that may materially depress the result.

contradiction_text: Hidden-test benchmark performance is used as the main autonomy measure, but revealing all test cases sharply improves Claude-Sonnet-4.5 results, showing that outcomes depend substantially on feedback visibility rather than only on the ability to generate the target repository.
related_claim_texts: Reported end-to-end performance remains limited in this setting, with the best overall pass rate at 40.2% for Claude-Sonnet-4.5 using Claude Code, while GPT-5 is reported at 21.7%.

{'The paper’s own results suggest that execution dynamics and task visibility materially affect outcomes': 'GPT-5 and Qwen3-Thinking show high non-finish or early-stop rates, task_tracker usage is strongly correlated with performance, and revealing all test cases substantially improves Claude-Sonnet-4.5 results.'}
disclosure: The article should keep visible that hidden-test scores measure a compound of coding ability, search, and evaluative-feedback constraints; the revealed-test gain does not erase difficulty, but it does limit simple readings of the hidden-test result.
